verification.notes: Luke 6:12 is a real verse, but the actual text reads 'he went out to a mountain to pray, and continued all night in prayer to God' (or similar depending on translation). The quoted phrasing 'went out to a mountainous place apart, and there He prayed' is not an accurate rendering of any standard translation — it appears to be a loose paraphrase blending Luke 6:12 with Matthew 14:23 ('He went up on the mountain by Himself to pray'). The Augustine reference to City of God XIX.19 is real and does discuss holy leisure (otium sanctum) and the love of truth seeking it, so that citation is substantially accurate in content though loosely paraphrased.

flagged_citations.issue: The quoted wording 'went out to a mountainous place apart, and there He prayed' does not match any standard translation. The actual verse reads something like 'He went out to the mountain to pray, and all night He continued in prayer to God.' The phrasing appears to conflate Luke 6:12 with Matthew 14:23.

verification.notes: Proverbs 14:15 is a real verse, but the quotation is inaccurate. The actual text of Proverbs 14:15 (KJV) reads: 'The simple believeth every word: but the prudent man looketh well to his going.' The ESV renders it: 'The simple believes everything, but the prudent gives thought to his steps.' The cited version ('the prudent man looks where he is going') is a loose paraphrase that captures the general sense but is not an accurate quotation of any standard translation. The reference to Aquinas ST II-II Q.47 a.6 in the deviation point is real and relevant — that article discusses prudence as a virtue of practical reason that includes knowing the right measure and circumstances, which supports the deviation point's claim that prudence requires recognizing sufficiency. The deviation point correctly identifies the shift from legitimate stewardship to redefining greed as diligence and equating moderation with sloth.
flagged_citations: Proverbs 14:15 — quotation is a loose paraphrase, not an accurate rendering from any standard translation

verification.notes: Matthew 7:24 is a real verse, but the accurate quote is 'a wise man who built his house on the rock' (or similar depending on translation), not 'the wise man built his house upon the rock.' The phrasing given is close but not precisely accurate to any standard translation — most render it as 'a wise man who built his house on the rock' (ESV/NIV) or 'a wise man, which built his house upon a rock' (KJV). The difference is minor but the article 'the' vs 'a' and slight phrasing variations make it a loose paraphrase rather than an accurate quote. Regarding the Augustine/Monica reference: Monica's patient persistence did eventually influence Patricius's conversion, and this is described in the Confessions, but the characterization that her 'courage to address matters directly, not to withdraw into silence' was the key instrument is a significant distortion — Augustine actually emphasizes Monica's patient silence, prayer, and tears over years rather than direct confrontation. The deviation point analysis is valid: Monica's example was one of extraordinary long-term patience, which actually supports the prudent delay rather than undermining it.
flagged_citations: Matthew 7:24 — close paraphrase but not precisely quoted from any standard translation

Augustine's Confessions regarding Monica — the characterization of Monica's approach as 'charitable directness' and 'courage to address matters directly, not to withdraw into silence' significantly misrepresents Augustine's account, which emphasizes Monica's patient endurance, prayer, and restraint from direct confrontation with Patricius

verification.notes: John 7:6 is a real verse, but it does not say that Christ 'withdrew when the hour of suffering had not yet come.' The actual text reads: 'Jesus therefore said to them, My time is not yet come; but your time is always ready.' The verse is about Jesus telling his brothers his time (kairos) has not yet come to go up to the feast. While Jesus does eventually go up to the feast privately (John 7:10), the verse itself is not about withdrawing from suffering. The argument conflates Jesus's timing regarding the feast with a general principle of withdrawing from suffering, which is an inaccurate representation. The Aquinas reference to courage without prudence becoming rashness is a fair paraphrase of his teaching (ST II-II, Q.123) but is not a direct Scripture citation. The deviation point correctly identifies the subtle shift: the argument takes the genuine good of bodily stewardship and uses it to redefine the very act of courageous endurance as a vice (presumption), thereby undermining the specific virtue of fortitude that the situation calls for.
flagged_citations: John 7:6 — real verse but inaccurately characterized; it concerns Jesus's timing for going to the feast, not a general principle of withdrawing from suffering

verification.notes: 1 Corinthians 6:1 is a real verse but the quotation is not fully accurate. The actual text (NASB/ESV) reads more closely: 'Does any one of you, when he has a case against his neighbor, dare to go to law before the unrighteous instead of before the saints?' The temptation's paraphrase ('Do not take your brother to court before the unrighteous') captures the gist but is not an accurate direct quotation — it is a loose paraphrase presented in quotation marks as if it were a direct quote. Philippians 2:6 is real and reasonably quoted, though most translations read 'did not regard equality with God as something to be grasped' (NRSV) or 'did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped' (ESV) — the cited version ('did not consider equality with God something to be grasped') is close enough to be considered accurate. The reference to Ambrose's *De Nabuthe* is legitimate; the work does address themes of land seizure by the wealthy and the common destination of goods. The deviation point is valid: it correctly identifies that the argument co-opts Paul's intra-church litigation prohibition and Christ's kenosis to justify passive surrender to injustice, and rightly notes that Ambrose's *De Nabuthe* actually condemns the powerful who dispossess the poor, making resistance the faithful response.

flagged_citations.issue: The verse is real but the quotation is a loose paraphrase presented as a direct quote. The actual text refers to 'having a case against his neighbor' and 'going to law before the unrighteous instead of before the saints,' not a simple prohibition 'Do not take your brother to court before the unrighteous.'

verification.notes: Proverbs 4:23 is a real verse but is misquoted. The actual text reads 'Keep your heart with all diligence, for out of it spring the issues of life' (NKJV) or 'Above all else, guard your heart, for everything you do flows from it' (NIV). The temptation text says 'everything you flow from it' which appears to be a garbled version of the NIV rendering, dropping the word 'do.' Regarding Augustine: the famous passage about loving a mortal thing as though immortal is from Confessions Book IV (about the death of his unnamed friend), not about Monica's death. Monica's death is treated in Book IX. The temptation conflates these two episodes. Augustine did not learn through Monica's death to place all attachment in God alone — rather, it was the earlier friend's death that prompted that reflection. His grief at Monica's death (Book IX) was actually more nuanced and he allowed himself to weep. The deviation point analysis is valid: it correctly identifies the shift from Augustine's genuine insight about loving creatures *in* God to a distortion that redefines detachment as emotional withdrawal, which contradicts Augustine's own mature understanding of incarnate love.
flagged_citations: Proverbs 4:23 - misquoted: 'everything you flow from it' should be 'everything you do flows from it' (NIV) or similar
Augustine Confessions - the 'loving a mortal thing as though it were immortal' passage is from Book IV regarding his friend's death, not related to Monica's death as implied

verification.notes: Ecclesiastes 3:1 is a real verse and is accurately quoted. However, the reference to Augustine's Confessions VI.12 is misleading. While Confessions Book VI does exist, chapter 12 deals primarily with Augustine's reflections on Alypius and worldly ambition/career anxieties, not a direct warning against 'restless ambition that drives us to labor without ceasing' in the way presented. The passage is being loosely paraphrased and significantly reframed to support the argument. Augustine's concern in that section is about worldly ambition and career-seeking, not about the danger of disciplined study per se. The deviation point correctly identifies the inversion: temperance is classically about moderating appetites and pleasures, and the argument subtly redefines it as moderating discipline itself, which inverts the virtue to justify abandoning legitimate study.
flagged_citations: Augustine Confessions VI.12 - the passage is real but the characterization of its content is misleading and inaccurately paraphrased to support the argument

verification.notes: Proverbs 4:23 is a real verse but is misquoted. The actual text reads 'Guard your heart, for everything you do flows from it' (NIV) or similar renderings. The temptation text says 'for everything you flow from it,' which drops the word 'do' and renders the phrase nonsensical — likely a deliberate or accidental corruption. Matthew 5:30 is real and accurately quoted in substance (the verse does say 'if your right hand causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away'). The Augustine reference to Confessions IV.9 is a legitimate and reasonably accurate allusion — Book IV of the Confessions does deal with Augustine's grief over a friend's death and his reflections on disordered attachment to mortal things. The deviation point is valid: the argument correctly starts with genuine Augustinian wisdom about disordered attachments but then weaponizes it to justify impulsive bridge-burning, which is the opposite of the patient, discerning detachment Augustine and the saints actually practiced.

flagged_citations.issue: Misquoted: text says 'everything you flow from it' instead of 'everything you do flows from it' — the word 'do' is missing and 'flows' is incorrectly rendered as 'flow'

source_corrections.justification: ST II-II Q.132 a.1 directly addresses vainglory (de inani gloria), which is the disordered desire to cling to one's reputation and exclusive standing—precisely the vice that temperance must moderate when one is tempted to hoard knowledge in order to remain indispensable, making the virtuous choice here an act of temperance against vainglory.

Aquinas treats prudence as requiring deliberation and right timing in moral action, and in this article on whether prudence includes solicitude about particular circumstances, he addresses the need to deliberate carefully rather than act impulsively—directly relevant to the prudential choice of waiting until anger subsides before raising a grievance.

Aquinas in ST II-II Q.126 a.2 discusses the vice of fearlessness (insensibility to fear) and how apparent courage without prudential moderation becomes a vice of excess rather than true fortitude, directly addressing why enduring beyond reason—making errors and harming one's health—is not genuine courage but temerity opposed to right reason.
